Long-term care hospital (LTCH) NA Patient was not discharged from an inpatient facility [Go to M1021] (M1005) Inpatient Discharge Date (Most Recent): 04/01/2026 UK- Unknown Primary Reason for Home Health/Medical Necessity (POC Element): Nancy Vu is a 90-year-old female patient admitted to home health care due to end-stage renal disease requiring intermittent hemodialysis, chronic oxygen dependence at home, baseline dementia and is reported bedbound at home with family caretakers. The patient is considered homebound because she was brought by EMS after being found with a room air saturation <50% requiring BVM/NRB and subsequent continuous BiPAP and stat hemodialysis for hyperkalemia and volume overload. Objective findings supporting homebound status include: acute on chronic hypoxic respiratory failure, CT showing bilateral interlobular septal thickening/ground-glass opacities with small-moderate pleural effusions, documented Morganella morganii bacteremia (blood culture +), ongoing IV antibiotic therapy, anemia, troponinemia consistent with demand ischemia, and eGFR/creatinine consistent with ESRD. Functional and safety limitations clearly documented in PT/OT/SLP evaluations make independent community departure unsafe: PT/OT note dependence for all ADLs and mobility, use of a manual wheelchair at home, bilateral lower-extremity weakness, and need for 1:1 supervision for feeding due to visual impairment and cognitive deficits. Leaving the home would require multiple skilled interventions and substantial, taxing effort, transfer and mobilization with trained caregivers, transport while managing supplemental oxygen and IV antibiotic/dialysis access, and real-time clinical monitoring for hypoxia, hemodynamic instability, electrolyte shifts, and bacteremia, all of which will increase risk of decompensation if attempted without skilled support. Allowable, limited absences are recognized and should be restricted to medically necessary trips with appropriate skilled escort and monitoring; other brief, supervised outings only as tolerated. Given the combination of recent critical respiratory compromise requiring respiratory support, ongoing need for dialysis via an indwelling catheter, active bacteremia on antibiotics, significant mobility dependence with mechanical lift transfers, dementia with feeding supervision needs, and anemia/troponinemia requiring medical monitoring, these objective findings render the patient substantially unable to leave home without considerable and taxing effort and establish the medical necessity for skilled home health services to ensure safe care and prevent readmission. ALL DIAGNOSIS AND MEDICATIONS VERIFIED WITH THE PRIMARY CARE PHYSICIAN. Nancy Vu is a 90-year-old female patient admitted to home health care due to end-stage renal disease requiring intermittent hemodialysis, chronic oxygen dependence at home, baseline dementia and is reported bedbound at home with family caretakers. The patient is considered homebound because she was brought by EMS after being found with a room air saturation <50% requiring BVM/NRB and subsequent continuous BiPAP and stat hemodialysis for hyperkalemia and volume overload. Objective findings supporting homebound status include: acute on chronic hypoxic respiratory failure, CT showing bilateral interlobular septal thickening/ground-glass opacities with small-moderate pleural effusions, documented Morganella morganii bacteremia (blood culture +), ongoing IV antibiotic therapy, anemia, troponinemia consistent with demand ischemia, and eGFR/creatinine consistent with ESRD. Functional and safety limitations clearly documented in PT/OT/SLP evaluations make independent community departure unsafe: PT/OT note dependence for all ADLs and mobility, use of a manual wheelchair at home, bilateral lower-extremity weakness, and need for 1:1 supervision for feeding due to visual impairment and cognitive deficits. Leaving the home would require multiple skilled interventions and substantial, taxing effort, transfer and mobilization with trained caregivers, transport while managing supplemental oxygen and IV antibiotic/dialysis access, and real-time clinical monitoring for hypoxia, hemodynamic instability, electrolyte shifts, and bacteremia, all of which will increase risk of decompensation if attempted without skilled support. Allowable, limited absences are recognized and should be restricted to medically necessary trips with appropriate skilled escort and monitoring; other brief, supervised outings only as tolerated. Given the combination of recent critical respiratory compromise requiring respiratory support, ongoing need for dialysis via an indwelling catheter, active bacteremia on antibiotics, significant mobility dependence with mechanical lift transfers, dementia with feeding supervision needs, and anemia/troponinemia requiring medical monitoring, these objective findings render the patient substantially unable to leave home without considerable and taxing effort and establish the medical necessity for skilled home health services to ensure safe care and prevent readmission. Frequency and Duration: HH SN frequency: 2 times a week for 1 week, 1 time a week for 3 weeks for skilled observation and assessment, medication reconciliation, pain assessment, wound monitoring and management, infection control measures, diet, home safety and disease process education. HH PT frequency: For evaluation and treatment for mobility, gait, transfer, establish therapeutic exercises, strengthening, home safety and fall prevention techniques training effective on week of 04/05/2026. Home Oxygen Therapy Order: Continuous O2 at 2 LPM via nasal cannula for shortness of breath. Goals: 1) Patient will maintain skin integrity with no new pressure injuries with ongoing repositioning and skin checks documented within 4 weeks. 2) Patient will have oxygenation maintained with SpO2 >92% on prescribed home O2 (2 L/min) at rest and during transfers, with titration documented and no unplanned ED visits for hypoxemia within 4 weeks. 3) Patient will attend all scheduled hemodialysis sessions and demonstrate stable post-dialysis vitals with documentation of intradialytic complications within 4 weeks. 4) Patient will have fall risk mitigation measures in place and document zero falls requiring treatment, within 4 weeks. 5) Patient will have nursing-documented education provided on signs/symptoms that require urgent care (fever >100.4°F, new dyspnea, catheter bleeding/drainage, syncope) with caregiver teach-back demonstrating correct actions within 4 weeks. She was referred for home health care on April 1, 2026, after being discharged from a short-stay acute hospital (IPPS) on the same date. The attending physician overseeing her care is Dr. Julie Wu, and the assessment for start of care was completed by registered nurse Bright VU, NANCY I 116293 I DOB: 07/12/1935 I N: 04/04/2026 18 of 22 Dizon on April 4, 2026. The patient's preferred language is Vietnamese, which may necessitate language support during care coordination. Nancy has been determined to be homebound based on several criteria. She experiences significant limitations in mobility due to a combination of conditions, including dyspnea on minimal exertion and dementia. Her inability to leave home is compounded by a medical contraindication to do so, as it would pose a risk to her health. Nancy is effectively bedbound and unable to tolerate standing or walking for extended periods. She exhibits impaired balance, which increases her risk of falls, and requires assistance to ambulate, often relying on walls and furniture for support. Leaving home is a considerable effort for her, often leading to exhaustion and the need for extended rest periods. The primary reason for Nancy's need for home health services is her diagnosis of acute respiratory failure with hypoxia (J96.01). This condition is critical and necessitates ongoing monitoring and support. Additionally, she has a secondary diagnosis of end-stage renal disease (N18.6) and is dependent on renal dialysis, which further complicates her health status. Other notable comorbidities include hypertension and unclassified dementia. A comprehensive head-to-toe assessment reveals several key findings. Cardiopulmonary assessment shows Nancy is experiencing shortness of breath and has a respiratory rate of 20 breaths per minute, which is concerning. Gastrointestinal function appears stable, but her nutritional status is precarious, as she is on a therapeutic diet designed to manage her renal condition and is unable to feed herself without assistance. The integumentary system shows no signs of pressure ulcers or injuries, but she is at high risk due to her immobility and health conditions. Psychosocially, Nancy exhibits signs of confusion and requires assistance with decision-making. She has a brief interview for mental status (BIMS) score of 8, indicating significant cognitive impairment. While she is alert to person, her orientation to time and place is severely impaired. She lives with other individuals who are providing care, but her dependence on them for daily activities is substantial. Vital signs taken during the assessment indicate that Nancy's temperature is 98.1°F, pulse is 84 beats per minute, and blood pressure is 174/77 mmHg. These readings suggest potential cardiovascular concerns that need to be monitored closely. Her cognitive function is compromised, requiring low-stimulus environments to minimize confusion and anxiety. In terms of activities of daily living (ADLs), Nancy requires assistance for most tasks. She can dress her upper body independently if clothing is prepared for her but needs help with her lower body. Bathing and toileting require substantial assistance, and she is currently bedfast, unable to ambulate or transfer without help. Her functional ability is categorized as dependent across various domains, including self-care and cognitive tasks. Nancy's mobility is significantly limited; she relies on a walker due to her poor balance and dyspnea with minimal exertion. Her current nursing and rehabilitative needs will focus on improving her strength and mobility while ensuring safety during transfers. The care plan will also address her nutritional needs, given her dependence on others for feeding. Socially, Nancy's living arrangements involve caregivers who provide essential support, although their capacity to assist may be limited due to their own circumstances. The care team will need to coordinate with these caregivers to ensure that Nancy's needs are met adequately. A review of Nancy's medications reveals that she is currently taking multiple prescriptions, including high-risk medications such as antibiotics. Close monitoring for potential drug interactions and compliance is crucial, as she requires reminders to take her medications correctly. The patient's nutritional health screen indicates she has made adjustments to her diet due to her health conditions, and she is categorized as having good nutritional status despite her limitations. In summary, Nancy Vu's medical necessity for skilled services in home health care is clear. She requires frequent monitoring of her respiratory status, assistance with ADLs, management of her complex medication regimen, and coordination of care related to her numerous health conditions. The recommended visit frequency for her care is at least three times a week, ensuring that her needs are addressed effectively and that she receives the support necessary for her recovery and quality of life.
